abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong program repair performance but often suffer from over-editing, where excessive modifications overwrite correct code and hinder bug localization. We systematically quantify its impact and introduce precise repair task, which maximizes reuse of correct code while fixing only buggy parts. Building on this insight, we propose PRepair, a framework that mitigates over-editing and improves repair accuracy. PRepair has two components: Self-Breaking, which generates diverse buggy programs via controlled bug injection and min-max sampling, and Self-Repairing, which trains models with Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (EA-GRPO) using an edit-aware reward to encourage minimal yet correct edits. Experiments show that PRepair improves repair precision by up to 31.4% under $\mathrm{fix}_1@1$, a metric that jointly considers repair correctness and extent, and significantly increases decoding throughput when combined with speculative editing, demonstrating its potential for precise and practical code repair.

citations.fullCitation: Zhihong Shao, Peiyi Wang, Qihao Zhu, Runxin Xu, Junxiao Song, Xiao Bi, Haowei Zhang, Mingchuan Zhang, Y. K. Li, Y. Wu, and Daya Guo. 2024. Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models. Preprint, arXiv:2402.03300.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the reinforcement learning algorithm that serves as the foundation for the PRepair framework. The main paper's core method, Edit-Aware GRPO (EA-GRPO), is a direct modification of GRPO designed to penalize excessive edits.
citations.title: Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

citations.fullCitation: Niklas Muennighoff, Qian Liu, Armel Zebaze, Qinkai Zheng, Binyuan Hui, Terry Yue Zhuo, Swayam Singh, Xiangru Tang, Leandro von Werra, and Shayne Longpre. 2023. Octopack: Instruction tuning code large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2308.07124.
citations.justification: This work is central to the paper as it introduces the HumanEval-Fix benchmark, one of the key datasets used for evaluation. It also represents a primary example of prior methods that optimize for correctness alone, which the PRepair paper identifies as a cause of the 'over-editing' problem it aims to solve.
citations.title: Octopack: Instruction tuning code large language models

citations.fullCitation: Mark Chen, Jerry Tworek, Heewoo Jun, Qiming Yuan, Henrique Ponde de Oliveira Pinto, Jared Kaplan, Harri Edwards, Yuri Burda, Nicholas Joseph, Greg Brockman, Alex Ray, Raul Puri, Gretchen Krueger, Michael Petrov, Heidy Khlaaf, Girish Sastry, Pamela Mishkin, Brooke Chan, Scott Gray, and 39 others. 2021. Evaluating large language models trained on code. arXiv preprint arXiv:2107.03374.
citations.justification: This paper is foundational to the evaluation methodology, as it introduced the pass@k metric. The main paper uses pass@k to measure repair correctness and proposes its own novel metric, fix_p@k, which extends the concept of pass@k to jointly consider correctness and edit cost.
citations.title: Evaluating large language models trained on code

citations.fullCitation: Yunhui Xia, Wei Shen, Yan Wang, Jason Klein Liu, Huifeng Sun, Siyue Wu, Jian Hu, and Xiaolong Xu. 2025. Leetcodedataset: A temporal dataset for robust evaluation and efficient training of code llms. Preprint, arXiv:2504.14655.
citations.justification: This citation is important as it provides the dataset of Python programming tasks used in the 'Self-Breaking' stage to generate the training data. The effectiveness of the PRepair framework for Python repair is directly dependent on the quality and nature of this source dataset.
citations.title: Leetcodedataset: A temporal dataset for robust evaluation and efficient training of code llms

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: QiMeng-PRepair: Precise Code Repair via Edit-Aware Reward Optimization

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Changxin Ke, Rui Zhang, Jiaming Guo, Yuanbo Wen, Di Huang, Zidong Du, Xing Hu, and Qi Guo from the State Key Lab of Processors, Institute of Computing Technology, CAS. Additional authors include Li Ding from the Institute of Microelectronics, CAS, and Changxin Ke, Li Ding, Shuo Wang, Xuyuan Zhu, Xiong Peng, and Yunji Chen (corresponding author) from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Automated program repair is a recognized area of research, with increasing attention in the context of Large Language Models (LLMs). Traditional approaches often involve multi-stage pipelines, encompassing error localization, correction, and validation. More recent trends, driven by the emergence of coding assistants, explore end-to-end LLM-based methods through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) to achieve program repair.

However, existing LLM-based repair models frequently focus on optimizing for repair correctness as the primary objective. This singular focus can lead to a phenomenon termed "over-editing," where models tend to regenerate substantial portions of code rather than identifying and minimally correcting specific buggy elements. Over-editing has implications for bug localization, code maintainability, and the cognitive load on developers during code review. This paper positions itself as addressing this identified limitation by introducing the concept of "precise repair," aiming to maximize the reuse of correct code while exclusively fixing faulty components. The work distinguishes itself by integrating edit-awareness into the training objective of LLMs, specifically addressing the challenges of data scarcity for precise repairs and the preservation of correct code during model optimization. It also introduces a new evaluation metric to quantify this dual objective of correctness and minimal edits.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of this research is to develop a framework that mitigates the problem of "over-editing" in Large Language Model (LLM)-based code repair, thereby enhancing both repair accuracy and the precision of modifications. The motivation for this work stems from several observed limitations and challenges associated with current LLM-driven program repair:

*   **Prevalence of Over-editing:** Existing LLMs, when trained primarily for repair correctness, often regenerate large sections of code. This behavior, termed "over-editing," implies that models may not deeply understand the semantic context to perform targeted corrections.
*   **Detrimental Effects of Over-editing:**
    *   **Limited Bug Localization:** Over-editing obscures the precise location of the original bug, hindering effective error diagnosis and understanding.
    *   **Reduced Maintainability:** Extensive and unnecessary code rewrites can disrupt the original code structure and style, making the repaired code harder to understand, integrate, and maintain for human developers.
    *   **Increased Review Burden:** Developers are tasked with reviewing not only the correctness of the fix but also the extent of modifications, which can be substantial and time-consuming if over-editing occurs.
    *   **Inefficient Inference:** Over-editing can negatively impact the efficiency of inference mechanisms such as speculative decoding, which rely on the reuse of existing code segments.
*   **Definition of Precise Repair:** The authors advocate for "precise repair," defined as maximizing the reuse of correct logic within the original buggy code while performing minimal, targeted fixes only to the erroneous parts. This approach aims to preserve the codebase's integrity and reduce developer effort.
*   **Challenges in Achieving Precise Repair:**
    *   **Data Scarcity:** Training models for precise repair requires a dataset of buggy code that retains substantial correct logic while containing localized, fixable faults. Such realistic, high-quality data is noted as scarce.
    *   **Preservation of Correct Code during Training:** It is challenging to incorporate mechanisms into the training process that make the model explicitly aware of the magnitude of its edits, guiding it to preserve correct code segments while still effectively identifying and fixing bugs.

To address these motivations and challenges, the research sets out to develop the PRepair framework, aiming to guide models towards performing precise repairs. This involves generating suitable training data and introducing a training objective that explicitly balances repair correctness with the extent of modifications. The ultimate goal is to improve the practical utility of LLMs as code repair assistants by making their outputs more focused, easier to review, and more efficient to generate.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The PRepair framework addresses the precise code repair task through a two-stage approach: Self-Breaking for data generation and Self-Repairing with Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (EA-GRPO) for model training. The methodology also includes a novel metric design for evaluation and integrates with speculative editing for inference efficiency.

**4.1. Problem Formulation and Edit Cost Definition**
Code is modeled as a sequence of lines. Program repair involves line-level insertions, deletions, and substitutions to transform a buggy program (X) into a corrected one (Y). To quantify the extent of modifications, the paper introduces **Edit Cost ($D_{EC}$)**, calculated as the Levenshtein distance between the buggy code and the corrected code, normalized by the number of lines in the source program. This normalization enables fair comparison across programs of varying sizes.

**4.2. Observations on Over-editing**
An empirical study using Python and Verilog code repair tasks showed that when models are trained with correctness-only rewards (using naive GRPO), repair accuracy improves, but the $D_{EC}$ (edit cost) substantially increases. This indicated that models learn to "hit" a correct solution by making large modifications rather than understanding and precisely fixing errors, motivating the need for explicit edit-awareness.

**4.3. Metric Design ($fix_p@k$)**
To evaluate both repair correctness and the extent of modifications, the paper proposes $fix_p@k$. This metric jointly considers whether a repair is correct and if its edit cost is within an acceptable ratio ($p$) of the theoretical minimum edit cost. The parameter $p$ defines the tolerance level for repair cost. A repair is considered "fixed" if it passes all unit tests ($correct_i$) AND its normalized edit cost relative to the golden fix ($D_{EC}(X, Y') / D_{EC}(X, Y)$) is less than or equal to $p$. The overall $fix_p@k$ is computed using an unbiased estimation method by sampling $n$ candidates.

**4.4. PRepair Framework**

The framework consists of two main stages:

*   **Self-Breaking:**
    *   **Objective:** To generate a large-scale dataset of high-quality, diverse buggy programs that mimic realistic scenarios where correct logic is largely preserved.
    *   **Process:** Given a program description and its golden code, an LLM is prompted to inject bugs into the golden code. This process generates a set of candidate buggy programs.
    *   **Min-Max Sampling Strategy:** To ensure diversity and avoid over-concentration on similar bug patterns, a subset of these buggy programs is selected. This strategy minimizes the maximum pairwise similarity (defined as $1 - D_{EC}(X_i, X_j)$) among the sampled buggy programs, encouraging broad bug coverage.

*   **Self-Repairing with EA-GRPO:**
    *   **Objective:** To train the LLM to perform precise code repairs on the buggy programs generated in the Self-Breaking stage.
    *   **Mechanism:** Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (EA-GRPO), an extension of GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization).
    *   **Dynamic Edit-Aware Reward Shaping:** Unlike correctness-only rewards, EA-GRPO incorporates an edit penalty that is applied dynamically.
        *   **Group Accuracy Threshold ($\alpha$):** The edit penalty is only activated when the average repair accuracy ($Acc_G$) of a group of generated candidate repairs exceeds a predefined threshold $\alpha$. This ensures that minimal edits are encouraged only after a baseline level of correctness is achieved, preventing premature optimization for minimal edits at the expense of functionality.
        *   **Standardized Edit Penalty ($P_G$):** For correct samples within groups that meet the accuracy threshold, a penalty is calculated based on the standardized $D_{EC}$ of the generated repair relative to the mean and standard deviation of $D_{EC}$ for other correct samples in the group. A sigmoid function bounds this penalty.
        *   **Reward Design ($R_G$):** The final reward for a sample is 1 minus a scaled version of the edit penalty (controlled by coefficient $\beta$) if the sample is correct and the group accuracy threshold is met; otherwise, it is 0. This reward function requires only the buggy input and the generated output, not the golden fix, for edit cost calculation.

**4.5. Speculative Edits (Inference Acceleration)**
The paper integrates PRepair with Prompt Lookup Decoding, a speculative decoding method, to accelerate inference. This method reuses the input prompt (buggy code) as a draft through N-gram matching. The core insight is that PRepair's objective of minimizing $D_{EC}$ directly increases the acceptance rate of these speculative edits. A theoretical derivation shows that a reduction in edit cost (D) leads to a non-linear increase in decoding throughput (T), meaning smaller edits directly translate to faster inference.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments were conducted on Qwen2.5-Coder-3B and Qwen2.5-Coder-7B models, using Python (HumanEval-Fix) and Verilog (QiMeng-CodeV-R1-derived) benchmarks, and compared against prompt engineering, GRPO, GPT4, and Gemini2.0-flash.

*   **Training is Necessary for Precise Repair:** Prompt engineering, even with specific instructions for minimal changes, showed limited and often unstable improvements in $fix_p@k$ (repair precision) and could degrade pass@k (correctness) for some models (e.g., GPT4 on Verilog). Commercial LLMs (GPT4, Gemini2.0-flash) exhibited significantly lower $fix_1@1$ compared to PRepair-trained models (e.g., 19.10% lower and 17.84% lower respectively for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B on Python), indicating that specialized training like EA-GRPO is required to achieve high precision.

*   **Enhanced Repair Precision and Correctness:**
    *   **Reduced Over-editing:** EA-GRPO substantially improved repair precision across both languages. Specifically, $fix_1@1$ increased by up to 20.95% on Python and 31.41% on Verilog compared to the original models, demonstrating a significant reduction in unnecessary edits.
    *   **GRPO Degradation:** Models trained with naive GRPO (correctness-only reward) exhibited a severe degradation in $fix_p$ metrics, with $fix_1@1$ dropping from 36.70% to 8.49% on Verilog, confirming pronounced over-editing.
    *   **Correctness Maintenance/Improvement:** EA-GRPO generally maintained or improved repair correctness (pass@k) alongside precision gains. Qwen2.5-Coder-7B with EA-GRPO showed pass@1 improvements of 1.37% on Python and 0.29% on Verilog over GRPO. This suggests that encouraging minimal edits aids bug localization and overall repair effectiveness, rather than hindering it.

*   **Improved Cross-domain Generalization:** In cross-domain evaluations (e.g., trained on Python, evaluated on Verilog), EA-GRPO-trained models maintained stable repair correctness and significantly improved $fix_1@1$. In contrast, GRPO-trained models showed noticeable drops in both correctness and precision (e.g., pass@1 decreased by 8.31% and $fix_1@1$ by 26.50% when trained on Python and evaluated on Verilog), indicating that correctness-only optimization does not foster robust code understanding or bug localization across domains.

*   **Accelerated Inference with Speculative Edits:** EA-GRPO significantly increased the draft token acceptance rate during speculative decoding due to its reduced edit cost. This led to a notable improvement in decoding throughput, reaching up to 15% faster inference. Conversely, GRPO's exacerbation of over-editing resulted in a throughput degradation of up to 35%, highlighting the practical value of precise repair for real-world code assistants.

*   **Ablation Study of EA-GRPO Parameters:** The ablation study confirmed the importance of the Group Accuracy Threshold ($\alpha$) and the penalty coefficient ($\beta$) in EA-GRPO. Optimal settings of these parameters were shown to be crucial for balancing repair correctness and minimal edits, with suboptimal values leading to degraded performance in either precision or correctness.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The PRepair framework addresses a practical limitation in the application of Large Language Models (LLMs) for automated program repair, specifically the issue of "over-editing." The work introduces several contributions with implications for both research and practical software development:

*   **Enhanced Practicality of LLM-based Repair:** By enabling precise and minimal code edits, PRepair makes LLM-generated fixes more aligned with human development practices. This reduces the cognitive load on developers during code review, as they confront fewer unnecessary modifications, thereby increasing the usability and trustworthiness of LLM-powered coding assistants. This directly impacts code maintainability by preserving the original structure and style.
*   **Novel Evaluation Metric:** The introduction of $fix_p@k$ provides a new standard for evaluating program repair models, moving beyond correctness-only metrics to encompass the crucial aspect of modification extent. This metric encourages the development of models that are not only accurate but also perform targeted, minimal changes.
*   **Implicit Bug Localization:** The methodology, particularly the edit-aware reward optimization, appears to guide models toward an implicit ability to localize bugs. By penalizing extensive changes when a correct fix can be achieved with fewer edits, models are encouraged to focus their attention on the truly buggy parts of the code. This is a significant step towards more interpretable and robust repair mechanisms.
*   **Improved Generalization:** The demonstrated improvements in cross-domain generalization suggest that PRepair fosters a more fundamental understanding of code and bug patterns, making models more adaptable to new programming languages or contexts without extensive retraining.
*   **Increased Inference Efficiency:** The synergy between precise repair and speculative decoding offers a tangible performance advantage. By reducing the edit cost, PRepair models achieve higher acceptance rates for speculative tokens, leading to faster decoding throughput. This is critical for the real-time responsiveness required by interactive coding assistants.
*   **Data Generation Strategy:** The Self-Breaking stage, coupled with min-max sampling, provides a method for generating high-quality, diverse buggy code for training. This addresses the challenge of data scarcity for precise repair tasks, which is a common bottleneck in machine learning for software engineering.
*   **Foundation for Future Work:** The paper identifies areas for future research, such as automatic hyperparameter tuning for EA-GRPO and extending the framework to file-level or project-level code repair. This indicates that the core concepts introduced by PRepair have broader applicability within software development.

In summary, PRepair represents a step in making LLM-based code repair more practical, efficient, and developer-friendly by explicitly addressing the critical balance between correctness and the precision of modifications.
overview:
## The Challenge of Over-editing in Code Repair

Automated program repair (APR) has evolved significantly with the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs). These models can often identify and correct bugs in a wide range of programming languages, from Python to hardware description languages like Verilog. However, a persistent issue in LLM-based repair is "over-editing." When tasked with fixing a bug, many models do not just perform a surgical correction; instead, they rewrite large sections of the code, even those that were originally correct.

This behavior, while sometimes resulting in code that passes unit tests, presents several practical problems. Over-editing makes the code significantly harder for human developers to review, as the original logic and style are lost in the rewrite. It also obscures the location of the original bug, making it difficult to understand the root cause of the error. Furthermore, from a computational perspective, generating large amounts of new code is slower and less efficient than making targeted edits.

![Comparison of Over-editing vs Precise Repair](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05963v1/x3.png)
*Figure 1: A comparison between a vanilla LLM's tendency to over-edit code and the precise, targeted repair achieved by PRepair. Precise repair preserves the original structure while fixing only the faulty components.*

The research introduces **PRepair**, a framework designed to achieve "precise repair." The goal is to maximize the reuse of the original, correct logic while performing minimal, targeted fixes only on the erroneous parts. This approach aims to align model behavior with human development practices, where surgical fixes are preferred over complete rewrites for the sake of maintainability and clarity.

## Quantifying Precision: The Edit Cost and $fix_p@k$ Metric

To address over-editing, it is first necessary to quantify it. The researchers model code as a sequence of lines and define the **Edit Cost** ($D_{EC}$) as the Levenshtein distance between the buggy code and the corrected code, normalized by the number of lines in the source program.

If we represent the buggy code as $X$ and the corrected version as $Y$, the normalized edit cost is defined as:

$$D_{EC}(X, Y) = \frac{\text{Levenshtein}(X, Y)}{\max(1, \text{Lines}(X))}$$

Standard metrics like $pass@k$ only measure whether a model's output passes unit tests. To measure precision, the paper introduces a new metric called $fix_p@k$. This metric considers a repair successful only if it is both functionally correct and falls within an acceptable edit budget. Specifically, a repair is considered "fixed" if it passes all tests and its edit cost relative to the "golden" (ideal) fix is within a factor $p$.

The indicator function for a precise fix for a candidate $Y'$ is:

$$fix_p(X, Y, Y') = \mathbb{I}(\text{pass tests}) \wedge \mathbb{I}\left(\frac{D_{EC}(X, Y')}{D_{EC}(X, Y)} \leq p\right)$$

The overall $fix_p@k$ score is then calculated by sampling $n$ candidates and determining the probability that at least one of the $k$ selected samples is both correct and precise:

$$fix_p@k = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-m}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$

where $m$ is the number of candidates that satisfy the precision criteria. This metric forces models to compete not just on accuracy, but on the efficiency and minimalism of their solutions.

## The PRepair Framework: Self-Breaking and Data Diversity

One major hurdle in training models for precise repair is the scarcity of high-quality data. Most existing code repair datasets contain pairs of buggy and fixed code, but the bugs are often artificial or the fixes involve excessive changes. PRepair addresses this through a two-stage process: **Self-Breaking** and **Self-Repairing**.

### Self-Breaking for Data Generation
In the Self-Breaking stage, an LLM is used to generate its own training data. Given a correct "golden" program and its description, the model is prompted to inject realistic bugs into the code. This ensures the resulting buggy code is fundamentally similar to the correct version, retaining most of the original logic.

To prevent the model from generating repetitive or trivial bugs, the framework employs a **Min-Max Sampling** strategy. When the model generates multiple buggy versions of a program, the framework selects a subset that maximizes the diversity between them. This is achieved by minimizing the maximum pairwise similarity between sampled buggy programs, where similarity is defined as $1 - D_{EC}(X_i, X_j)$. This diversity is crucial for teaching the model to recognize and fix a wide variety of bug patterns.

![PRepair Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05963v1/x4.png)
*Figure 2: The two-stage PRepair framework. First, the Self-Breaking stage generates diverse buggy code. Second, the Self-Repairing stage uses EA-GRPO to train the model with an edit-aware reward system.*

## Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (EA-GRPO)

The core of PRepair's training is **Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (EA-GRPO)**. This is an extension of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm that optimizes models based on the relative performance of a group of generated samples.

Standard GRPO for code repair typically uses a binary reward: 1 if the code passes tests, and 0 otherwise. However, this often encourages over-editing because the model learns that more extensive rewrites are a "safer" way to pass the tests. EA-GRPO introduces a **Dynamic Edit-Aware Reward Shaping** mechanism to counteract this.

### Group Accuracy Threshold
To ensure the model doesn't prioritize minimal edits at the expense of actually fixing the bug, EA-GRPO uses a group accuracy threshold $\alpha$. The edit penalty is only activated for a group of samples if their average accuracy $Acc_G$ exceeds $\alpha$:

$$Acc_G = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m \mathbb{I}(Y'_i \text{ is correct})$$

If the group is struggling to find any correct solution, the model is encouraged to focus purely on correctness. Once the model becomes "confident" enough to generate correct solutions regularly, the edit penalty kicks in to refine those solutions.

### The Edit Penalty
For correct samples in high-accuracy groups, a standardized edit penalty $P_G$ is calculated. This penalty compares the edit cost of a specific sample $Y'_i$ to the average edit cost of other correct samples in the same group:

$$P_G(Y'_i) = \text{Sigmoid}\left(\frac{D_{EC}(X, Y'_i) - \text{mean}(D_{EC}^c)}{\text{std}(D_{EC}^c) + \epsilon}\right)$$

where $D_{EC}^c$ represents the edit costs of the correct samples in the group, and $\epsilon$ is a small constant for stability. This relative penalty ensures the model is always pushing to be more efficient than its own average performance. The final reward $R_G$ is scaled by a coefficient $\beta$:

$$R_G(Y'_i) = 1 - \beta \cdot P_G(Y'_i)$$

This reward structure allows the model to learn bug localization implicitly. To minimize edits while maintaining correctness, the model must learn exactly which lines are faulty and leave the rest untouched.

## Improving Inference with Speculative Edits

The benefits of precise repair extend beyond code quality to inference speed. PRepair integrates with **speculative decoding** (specifically Prompt Lookup Decoding) to accelerate code generation. 

Speculative decoding works by using a fast "drafting" mechanism to predict the next few tokens, which are then verified by the larger model in parallel. In the context of code repair, the buggy input code itself can serve as a draft. If the repair is precise (i.e., it reuses most of the input code), the acceptance rate of these draft tokens increases significantly. 

The research demonstrates a direct mathematical link: as the edit cost $D_{EC}$ decreases, the acceptance rate of draft tokens grows, leading to a non-linear increase in decoding throughput. Experiments showed that PRepair models achieved up to 15% faster inference compared to standard models, while models trained with naive GRPO (which over-edit) saw throughput drops of up to 35% because their drafts were constantly rejected.

![Inference Throughput and Acceptance Rate](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05963v1/x8.png)
*Figure 3: PRepair significantly increases the acceptance rate of draft tokens during speculative decoding, leading to higher throughput in tokens per second across both Verilog and Python.*

## Experimental Results and Generalization

The PRepair framework was evaluated on both general-purpose programming (Python) and specialized hardware description languages (Verilog). The results showed consistent improvements across several dimensions:

1.  **Precision Gains:** On the Python HumanEval-Fix benchmark, PRepair increased the $fix_1@1$ metric (which requires a perfect match in edit cost) by over 20% compared to base models. In Verilog, the improvement was even more pronounced, reaching 31%.
2.  **Maintaining Correctness:** Unlike simple prompt engineering, which often trades off accuracy for brevity, EA-GRPO maintained or slightly improved the overall $pass@k$ rates. This suggests that teaching the model to be precise actually helps it understand the code structure better.
3.  **Cross-Domain Robustness:** One of the most striking findings was PRepair's ability to generalize. Models trained on Python and then evaluated on Verilog (and vice-versa) maintained high precision. In contrast, models trained with naive GRPO showed a significant drop in performance when moved to a new domain, often reverting to extreme over-editing.
4.  **Implicit Localization:** Analysis of the model's attention maps revealed that PRepair-trained models focus more intently on the specific buggy lines during generation, whereas standard models distribute their attention more broadly, leading to unnecessary changes.

![Performance Comparison across Domains](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05963v1/x7.png)
*Figure 4: Comparing in-domain and cross-domain code repair performance. EA-GRPO (red) consistently outperforms naive GRPO (blue) in both correctness and precision, especially when generalizing to new programming languages.*

## Conclusion

The QiMeng-PRepair framework demonstrates that "more editing" does not equal "better repair." By introducing edit-awareness into the reinforcement learning loop and creating a high-quality synthetic dataset through Self-Breaking, PRepair produces models that are surgical, efficient, and developer-friendly. 

The introduction of the $fix_p@k$ metric provides a necessary benchmark for evaluating the real-world utility of coding assistants. As LLMs become more integrated into software development pipelines, the ability to perform precise, minimal modifications will be essential for maintaining codebases that remain readable, maintainable, and efficient to generate. PRepair offers a robust path forward for achieving this balance between functional correctness and structural integrity.

keyInsights: Optimizing LLMs for repair correctness alone leads to an "over-editing" phenomenon where models make extensive, unnecessary changes.
Incorporating dynamic, edit-aware reward shaping into policy optimization can guide LLMs to perform precise repairs while sustaining or improving overall correctness.
Precise code repair, characterized by minimal edits, inherently enhances inference efficiency in speculative decoding by increasing draft token acceptance rates.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) for code repair frequently "over-edit" by regenerating extensive code, hindering bug localization and maintainability.
Existing LLM-based repair methods struggle with data scarcity for precise, minimal fixes and lack mechanisms to preserve correct code during training.
Over-editing degrades code quality, increases developer cognitive load during review, and negatively impacts the efficiency of inference mechanisms like speculative decoding.
results: PRepair with EA-GRPO increased $fix_1@1$ by up to 20.95% (Python) and 31.41% (Verilog), demonstrating a marked reduction in over-editing compared to baseline models.
The framework preserved or enhanced repair correctness (pass@k) and showed improved cross-domain generalization, unlike naive correctness-only training which often led to degradation.
Precise repairs from EA-GRPO led to up to 15% faster inference throughput via speculative decoding, contrasting with throughput degradation observed from over-editing models.
solution: The PRepair framework employs a "Self-Breaking" stage to generate high-quality, diverse buggy programs with preserved correct logic.
An "Edit-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization" (EA-GRPO) is introduced, dynamically applying an edit penalty based on group accuracy to balance repair correctness with minimal modifications.
A new metric, $fix_p@k$, quantifies repair performance by simultaneously assessing correctness and the precision of modifications relative to the theoretical minimum edit cost.
summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences developed the PRepair framework to mitigate "over-editing" in large language model-based code repair, improving both the accuracy and precision of modifications. The framework, which utilizes a novel edit-aware reward optimization, increases repair precision ($fix_1@1$) by up to 31.41% on Verilog and accelerates inference by up to 15%.

title: QiMeng-PRepair: Precise Code Repair via Edit-Aware Reward Optimization
